description: Targa Resources is a U.S. midstream energy company that gathers, processes, transports, stores, fractionates, and markets natural gas, natural gas liquids, and related energy products.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: Targa Resources operates a large midstream energy platform focused on natural gas and natural gas liquids infrastructure. Its assets connect upstream production basins with downstream markets through gathering systems, processing plants, fractionation facilities, storage, transportation, and logistics networks.

The company sits in the less glamorous but highly consequential middle layer of the hydrocarbon economy: not usually the brand on the pump, but very much part of the machinery that keeps molecules moving. Its role is infrastructure-heavy, capital-intensive, and closely tied to U.S. natural gas and NGL production trends.

overview.title: Product And Service Footprint
paragraphs: The core product areas currently mapped for the registry are natural gas gathering and processing, plus NGL logistics. These cover the company’s work collecting raw gas, separating usable components, moving NGLs through pipelines and terminals, and supporting fractionation, storage, export, and marketing activity.

For Free The World purposes, Targa should be treated less like a consumer-facing energy company and more like a critical industrial logistics operator. The relevant comparison set is infrastructure, commodity transport, fossil-fuel dependency, and energy-system lock-in rather than apps, platforms, or direct digital services.

overview.title: Registry Relevance
paragraphs: Targa’s importance comes from its role in extending and optimizing fossil-fuel supply chains. Midstream firms can appear neutral because they provide transport and processing rather than extraction or combustion, but the practical effect is to make continued hydrocarbon production more efficient and commercially durable.

The registry should track Targa as part of the oil-and-gas midstream layer, with attention to open energy data, grid transparency, electrification, public-interest infrastructure planning, methane accountability, and alternatives that reduce dependence on private fossil-fuel logistics networks.
